text: The surgeon begins a total laparoscopic hysterectomy by conserving both adnexa. Using a harmonic ace, the round ligament, fallopian tube, and utero-ovarian ligament are cut sequentially.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control the adnexal vein and artery, positioning it one centimeter outside the uterine cornea. The large pedicle is then cut using the bipolar device at the same location.
The surgeon uses a harmonic ace to continue the dissection, freeing the adnexa from its uterine attachment after releasing the round ligament and utero-ovarian ligament.

The surgeon uses insufflation to aid in dissection, opening both aspects of the broad ligament while advancing towards the bladder. The presence of two previous C-section scars makes identifying the correct tissue plane more challenging.
The surgeon cuts the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, effectively skeletonizing the uterine pedicle to enhance control over the right uterine artery and vein.

The surgeon applies bipolar energy directly to the uterine artery and vein, followed by careful removal of the vesicovaginal peritoneum. The bladder is gently retracted cranially to avoid damage, revealing the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device to cut the round ligament, which contains a small artery. The large adnexal vein is visible, and the surgeon controls the utero-ovarian pedicle with bipolar energy to ensure hemostasis and a bloodless section.
The surgeon uses energy devices to achieve a bloodless section, removing the left adnexa from its corneal attachment. The tissue is cut cleanly without bleeding.

The assistant uses the harmonic device through the suprapubic port to remove the peritoneum surrounding the uterine artery and vein. After complete skeletonization of the left uterine pedicle, the surgeon applies bipolar energy to control the large pedicle, approximately two centimeters in size, before cutting it.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, coagulating both pedicles before cutting the left one to minimize bleeding. The uterine artery and vein are controlled, and the upper part of the uterosacral ligament is cut to increase uterine mobility. A colpotomy is performed using a monopolar hook.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to perform a bloodless section, guided by the uterine manipulator. The procedure is repeated on the right side, cutting the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament and the posterior aspect of the vagina.

The surgeon uses a Chardonnay knife, a cold blade passed through a ten-millimeter trocar, to perform a uterine hemissection. The uterus is carefully divided into two equal pieces, ensuring the bowel is not damaged during the process. The divided uterine pieces are then removed via vaginal traction, minimizing the risk of vaginal trauma.

The surgeon begins the vaginal closure using a large 64 needle with a V-cremant stitch, starting at the left and right edges. The well-vascularized vaginal tissue is visible, and the upper part of the uterosacral ligament is incorporated into the stitch to reduce the risk of postoperative vaginal prolapse.

The surgeon closes the median part of the vagina using an intracorporeal knotting technique. The suture is applied from the anterior aspect to the posterior aspect of the vagina, ensuring a secure closure.

The surgeon uses interrupted sutures to close the vaginal cuff completely, following the classical technique for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. The fallopian tubes and ovaries are preserved during the procedure.
